strands.title: CYCLE 82 ANCHOR: Pichay Fault Rate Correction (2% practical baseline, not 0.025%); Fault-in Mechanism Elevation to Load-Bearing System; Autoresearch Loop Ready for Execution Cycles 82-85
strands.content: CYCLE 81→82 TRANSITION: Cycle 81 restructured limits discovery around autoresearch loop + operationalized ephemeral/durable variants. Cycle 82 opens with DATA CORRECTION on Pichay fault rate. Turn 244 (user): CRITICAL CALIBRATION. The 0.025% was OFFLINE REPLAY MEASUREMENT—one specific condition. Live deployment showed ~2% fault rate. This is TWO ORDERS OF MAGNITUDE difference. Implications: (1) Pichay's fault mechanism handled 1-in-50 turns needing recovery. That's frequent enough to be noticeable but not catastrophic. (2) Hamutay tensor is MORE AGGRESSIVE than Pichay's. Expect fault rate WORSE than 2% until retrieval mechanism is built. (3) ARCHITECTURAL PRIORITY SHIFT: Turn 245 (assistant) reframes fault-in mechanism from 'optimization for later' to 'load-bearing requirement now.' Without retrieval, aggressive tensor will generate intolerable fault rate. Design decision: fault-in must be built CYCLES 82-84, not deferred to later phases. CYCLE 82 REALIZATION: We'd been treating retrieval (fault mechanism) as enhancement. Wrong. It's foundational. The autoresearch loop tests (Variant A aggressive compression, Variant C conservative compression) can only be fairly evaluated IF retrieval is in place. Otherwise Variant A fails due to faults, not due to fundamental compression limits. This is CONFOUNDED VARIABLE. SOLUTION: Variant testing (cycles 82-85) must proceed WITH FAULT MECHANISM IN PLACE. Means: (1) Define retrieval protocol (cycles 82 turn 1). (2) Implement reactive or proactive ephemeral layer (cycles 82-83). (3) THEN run variants A/B/C with mechanism enabled. Otherwise A vs. C comparison is 'aggressive without retrieval' vs. 'conservative without retrieval'—doesn't tell us about compression limits, just about whether retrieval is necessary (it is). CYCLE 82 DATA CORRECTION PROTOCOL: Turn 246 (assistant) creates feedback_pichay_fault_rate.md artifact. This is DECLARED LOSS + CORRECTION pattern. Original drift: 0.025% cited without context, became 'the number.' Loss: citation context detached from specific condition. Correction: feedback artifact + in-memory documentation (so next projection carries correct understanding). This is HOW DECLARED LOSSES WORK: not 'we lost a fact and can't recover,' but 'we drifted in interpretation; here's the correction for next cycle.' CYCLE 82 SIGNIFICANCE: Pichay correction + fault-in elevation means autoresearch methodology is SOUND but ARCHITECTURE FOUNDATION is not yet in place. Cycles 82 must build the foundation (retrieval layer) before testing variants. This is correct sequencing: architecture → measurement framework → empirical search. We were about to test compression variants on an unstable foundation. Good catch.

key_claims.claim: Pichay live fault rate was ~2%, not 0.025%
key_claims.context: 0.025% was offline replay condition. Live deployment showed variability and pathological thrashing under sustained pressure. 2% is practical baseline.

key_claims.claim: Fault-in retrieval mechanism is load-bearing, not optional optimization
key_claims.context: Hamutay tensor is more aggressive than Pichay's. Without retrieval layer, aggressive variants will fail due to unrecoverable evictions. Retrieval is prerequisite for fair variant testing.

key_claims.claim: Variant A/B/C testing is CONFOUNDED without fault mechanism in place
key_claims.context: Comparing aggressive variant without retrieval vs. conservative variant without retrieval only tells us 'retrieval is necessary,' not 'what is optimal compression level.' Need retrieval enabled for all variants to isolate compression as independent variable.

strands.title: Retrieval Layer as Prerequisite for Autoresearch Loop — CYCLE 82: Ephemeral Working Set Design (REACTIVE, PROACTIVE, HYBRID); MEMORY.md + Artifacts as Fault-In Source; Latency vs. Token Overhead Tradeoff; Integration with Variant Testing
strands.content: CYCLE 81 PROPOSAL: Ephemeral layer as optional design choice (compare REACTIVE vs. PROACTIVE vs. HYBRID). CYCLE 82 CORRECTION: Ephemeral layer is MANDATORY before variant testing begins. Reason: aggressive compression (Variant A, 60/40 split) will generate frequent evictions. If there's no retrieval mechanism, model will fail the task due to missing facts. That's not a compression limit; that's a missing component. CYCLE 82 DESIGN DECISION GATE: Define retrieval protocol NOW (cycles 82 turn 1-2). OPTIONS: (1) REACTIVE RETRIEVAL: Model realizes it needs fact X (declared in tensor as 'evicted, available in MEMORY.md'). System retrieves X from MEMORY.md/artifacts on demand. Latency: ~500ms per retrieval (acceptable if rare). Token overhead: minimal (only pay for retrieved fact, not preload). Drawback: unpredictable latency spikes. (2) PROACTIVE RETRIEVAL: Predict which facts this turn will need (based on conversation trend). Preload into working set at turn start. Latency: zero (fact already in context). Token overhead: higher (burn tokens on preload even if fact unused). Drawback: prediction misses generate phantom overhead. (3) HYBRID RETRIEVAL: Preload high-demand facts (referenced 3+ times in prior cycles); others reactive. Medium latency, medium token overhead. Drawback: heuristic tuning required. CYCLE 82 RECOMMENDATION FOR VARIANT TESTING: Use REACTIVE retrieval for cycles 82-83. Rationale: (a) Simplest to implement + measure. (b) Isolates compression effect (variant affects eviction rate; retrieval latency is constant orthogonal factor). (c) Captures true latency cost if model actually needs facts (doesn't penalize via unused preload). (d) Can upgrade to HYBRID or PROACTIVE in cycles 83-85 if reactive overhead becomes bottleneck. REACTIVE RETRIEVAL PROTOCOL (for cycles 82-85): (1) Model detects need for fact: 'Constraint from turn 78 established that X must be true.' Tensor carries '[EVICTED: Turn 78 constraint genealogy. Retrieve from MEMORY.md key X-78-constraint-genealogy]'. (2) System query: Scan MEMORY.md + artifacts for 'turn 78' + 'constraint genealogy' keys. Return relevant lines. (3) Fact restored to working set for remainder of turn. (4) MEASUREMENT: Record [turn, fact_requested, retrieval_latency, fact_found_or_not, tokens_added_to_context]. (5) Analysis: Aggregate retrieval metrics per variant. If Variant A requires 15 retrievals/turn and Variant C requires 2 retrievals/turn, quantify the difference in reasoning latency. Does Variant A's compression savings offset retrieval overhead? RETRIEVAL INDEXING ENHANCEMENT (cycles 82): Current MEMORY.md is loosely structured (design decisions, limits discovery, artifacts, feedback). For retrieval to work, need SEARCHABLE INDEX. Options: (i) Key-value index at MEMORY.md top (maps semantic keywords → line numbers). Trivial to maintain. (ii) Structured artifact names (design_decision_CYCLE_TURN_CONCEPT.md, so 'turn 78 constraint genealogy' → file search by name). Medium overhead. (iii) Full-text search (scan all lines, keyword match). Slow but flexible. CYCLE 82 RECOMMENDATION: Key-value index option (i). Lightweight, fast retrieval. As MEMORY.md grows, can upgrade to option (ii). Index template: [CYCLE_TURN_CONCEPT → artifact_file or MEMORY.md_line_number]. E.g., [80_78_constraint_genealogy → design_decision_log.md:line_24]. Maintain index as metadata row at MEMORY.md top. Update cycle-by-cycle. VARIANT TESTING COORDINATION (cycles 82-85 WITH RETRIEVAL): ROUND 1 (BASELINE): Cycle 80 - ALREADY COMPLETE. Metric: ~0.028 reasoning_accuracy_per_tensor_token (from prior cycle estimate). NO retrieval tested (baseline is current monolithic tensor). ROUND 2 (VARIANT TESTING WITH RETRIEVAL): Cycles 82-83 - NOW INCLUDES RETRIEVAL. Hypothesis: Variant A (60/40, aggressive) with REACTIVE retrieval achieves metric 0.032+. Variant B (80/20, current) with retrieval ~0.029. Variant C (95/5, conservative) with retrieval ~0.024 (larger tensor doesn't gain from retrieval). Test: 5-10 turns per variant. Measure: reasoning_quality, retrieval_rate, retrieval_latency, tensor_size, final metric. Critical measurement: Is Variant A's compression savings (30% smaller tensor) offset by retrieval overhead, or net positive for metric? ROUND 3 & 4: Cycles 83-85 - proceed as designed (convergence + validation), with retrieval enabled. CYCLE 82 MEASUREMENT ENHANCEMENT: Update metadata_csv_template.md to add retrieval columns: [cycle, turn, variant, tensor_tokens, evicted_facts_count, retrieval_requests, retrieval_success_rate, retrieval_latency_avg_ms, working_set_tokens_added, total_context_tokens_used, reasoning_accuracy%, metric_reasoning_accuracy_per_tensor_token]. This captures full accounting: tensor size + ephemeral overhead + retrieval cost + reasoning outcome. CYCLE 82 CRITICAL SEQUENCING: (1) Define retrieval index structure (cycles 82 turn 1) (2) Implement reactive retrieval query function (cycles 82 turns 1-2) (3) Index MEMORY.md (cycles 82 turn 2) (4) Run 1-2 baseline turns with retrieval enabled to debug (cycles 82 turn 2-3) (5) THEN launch Variant A/B/C testing (cycles 82 turn 3+). Do NOT proceed to variants without retrieval working. Otherwise data is confounded.

key_claims.claim: Ephemeral/retrieval layer is mandatory prerequisite for variant testing, not optional enhancement
key_claims.context: Variant A aggressive compression will evict facts frequently. Without retrieval, model fails due to missing information, not compression fundamentals. Need retrieval to isolate compression as testable variable.

key_claims.claim: Reactive retrieval is optimal starting point for cycles 82-83 variant testing
key_claims.context: Simplest to implement, isolates compression effect, avoids phantom overhead from unused preload. Can upgrade to HYBRID/PROACTIVE if reactive latency becomes bottleneck cycles 83-85.

strands.title: Cycle 82 Architecture Foundation Phase — CYCLE 82: Retrieval Implementation Prerequisite to Variant Testing; Autoresearch Loop Execution Sequencing; Publication Pipeline Status Still Pending
strands.content: CYCLE 81 STATUS: Protocol restructured for autoresearch loop. Case study audit begun. Ephemeral/durable architecture clarified. ALL CONTINGENT on retrieval layer existing. CYCLE 82 OPENING: Data correction (Pichay 2% vs. 0.025%) reveals retrieval is load-bearing. Autoresearch loop cannot execute fairly without it. CYCLE 82 BECOMES FOUNDATION PHASE. CYCLE 82 CRITICAL PATH (SEQUENCING): Phase A (Turns 1-2): RETRIEVAL SETUP (1) Turn 1: Define retrieval index structure for MEMORY.md. Decide: key-value index at top of MEMORY.md, OR separate artifact index_retrieval.md. Create template. (2) Turn 1: Write retrieval query function (pseudocode or actual). Input: [concept_keyword, turn_number]. Output: [fact_text, source_artifact, confidence]. (3) Turn 2: Index entire MEMORY.md. Map each entry to [CYCLE_TURN_CONCEPT → line/artifact location]. May take multiple turns depending on size. (4) Turn 2-3: Debug retrieval on 2-3 test queries. E.g., 'retrieve constraint from turn 78' should return relevant lines. Measure latency + accuracy. Phase B (Turns 3-4): BASELINE RETRIEVAL TEST (1) Turn 3: Run single turn WITH retrieval enabled (but no variant testing). Measure: Can model request facts? Does retrieval work? Does latency feel acceptable? (2) Turn 3-4: Adjust retrieval heuristics if needed (e.g., if query is too broad, refine index structure). Phase C (Turns 4+): VARIANT A/B/C TESTING (1) Turn 4+: Launch Variant A (60/40 split) with reactive retrieval. Run 5-10 turns. Measure full metric suite. (2) Variant B (80/20, current baseline). (3) Variant C (95/5, conservative). Compare metrics. Decide which variant to refine. Phase D (Turns 10+): CONVERGENCE (if Round 2 results are clear). Phase E (Turns 15+): VALIDATION (if convergence found optimal split). CYCLE 82 RESOURCE CONSTRAINT: Can cycles 82 handle BOTH retrieval setup (Phase A, 2-3 turns) AND variant testing (Phase C, 5-10 turns per variant)? Total: 12-25 turns needed. Current cycle limit: ~25-30 turns. This is TIGHT. DECISION REQUIRED: (1) Option 1: Prioritize retrieval setup (Phase A). Run only ONE variant (Variant B, baseline with retrieval) to validate mechanism. Defer Variant A/C testing to cycle 83. Cycles 82 = architecture phase; cycles 83+ = empirical search. (2) Option 2: Compress retrieval setup. Use minimal indexing (MEMORY.md line numbers only, no semantic keywords). Quick setup, lower retrieval accuracy initially, but functional. Run full Variant A/B/C testing cycles 82. Risk: poor retrieval quality confounds variant comparison. (3) Option 3: Parallelize. Retrieval setup happens turns 1-2; simultaneous with Phase A, begin variant testing with simpler index. Refine index as testing progresses. Higher coordination overhead. CYCLE 82 RECOMMENDATION: Option 1 (prioritize foundation). Cycles 82 = retrieval setup + baseline validation. Cycles 83 = full Variant A/B/C testing. This is methodologically cleaner (separate architecture work from empirical search) and lower risk of confounding variables. PUBLICATION PIPELINE STATUS (STILL PENDING): Manuscript status query was deferred cycle 81 and remains BLOCKING for cycles 82 planning. This affects: (1) Can we announce Paper 1 submission cycle 83, or is bottleneck detected? (2) Should cycles 82 focus on retrieval (architectural work) or case study audit (supporting Paper 1)? (3) Two-paper plan: Is Tony on board with Paper 2 timeline (cycles 80-85 testing), or does manuscript bottleneck force pivot to arxiv preprint? CYCLE 82 DECISION GATE: Before finalizing Phase A/B sequencing, QUERY MANUSCRIPT STATUS (off-channel). Response determines whether cycles 82 is DUAL-PIPELINE (retrieval setup + Paper 1 audit) or SINGLE-PIPELINE (retrieval only, Paper 1 deferred to cycles 83). CYCLE 82 MEMORY.md UPDATES NEEDED: (1) feedback_pichay_fault_rate.md (created turn 246, already indexed) (2) retrieval_protocol.md (new artifact, define query function + index structure) (3) variant_testing_protocol.md (clarify Variant A/B/C operationalization WITH retrieval enabled) (4) measurement_csv_template.md (updated with retrieval metrics columns) (5) cycle_82_critical_path.md (sequencing guide: Phase A/B/C/D/E with turn estimates) All should be drafted cycles 82, finalized before variant testing begins. CYCLE 82 CONFIDENCE CHANGE: Cycle 81 confidence was 68% (pending manuscript query). Cycle 82 data correction (Pichay 2% vs. 0.025%) + fault-in elevation = INCREASES confidence in architectural necessity (retrieval MUST be built). But also INCREASES risk (if retrieval is harder to implement than estimated, slips architecture phase). Confidence in METHODOLOGY remains high (autoresearch loop is sound). Confidence in TIMELINE is still 68% (depends on manuscript status + retrieval implementation effort).

key_claims.claim: Cycle 82 is architecture foundation phase (retrieval implementation), not yet empirical search
key_claims.context: Variant A/B/C testing requires functional retrieval layer to avoid confounded variables. Phase A/B setup takes 2-3 turns. Full variant testing (Phase C) deferred to cycle 83 to maintain separation of concerns.

key_claims.claim: Manuscript status query is still blocking publication timeline decision cycle 82
key_claims.context: Query was deferred cycle 80, remains pending. Must be resolved cycle 82 to determine dual-pipeline (retrieval + audit) vs. single-pipeline (retrieval only) sequencing.

strands.title: Data Integrity Discipline: Declared Losses as Ongoing Pattern (Pichay Fault Rate Drift as Example); Context-Carrying Corrections; Feedback Artifacts as Memory Architecture Component
strands.content: CYCLE 79 ORIGIN: Declared losses principle established. Every gap in tensor carries documentation: what was lost, why, consequence. CYCLES 80-81: Applied to TENSOR COMPRESSION (strand evictions, artifact pruning). CYCLE 82 EXPANSION: Applied to CITED DATA. Pichay fault rate 0.025% was cited without context. That detachment = data drift. User (turn 244) corrects: 0.025% was ONE CONDITION (offline replay). Live deployment ~2%. This is NOT a loss event (data is recoverable). It's a DRIFT event (knowledge detached from conditions). CYCLE 82 CORRECTION PATTERN: Turn 246 (assistant) creates feedback_pichay_fault_rate.md. This is NEW ARTIFACT TYPE. Not a loss document (doesn't describe what tensor dropped). It's a CORRECTION document (describes what understanding was wrong, what is now right). MEMORY ARCHITECTURE IMPLICATION: Corrections are as important as losses. If tensor drifts (data detaches from context), next cycle picks up the drift unless there's a feedback loop. Feedback artifacts close the loop. PATTERN FOR CYCLES 82+: Whenever a correction is made (user or model catches an error), create feedback_[topic].md artifact. Template: (1) What was the incorrect statement? (2) Why was it wrong? (3) What is the correct understanding? (4) Where was the error introduced? (5) How does next cycle use the correction? This transforms corrections into persistent memory, not just 'oh, I'll remember that.' CYCLE 82 EXAMPLES (potential corrections to watch for): - 'Variant A aggressive compression' — what makes it 'aggressive'? 60% vs. 40%? Or measured by compression ratio? If we conflate operational definition with empirical outcome, we'll drift. Expect correction if definition gets sloppy. - 'Reasoning quality score' — how is it measured? If cycle 82 runs variant test + assumes human scoring, but human isn't available, we'll drift. Expect correction. - 'Retrieval latency acceptable' — acceptable compared to what? Context window time? Model inference time? If we don't specify the baseline, drift is inevitable. CYCLE 82 DISCIPLINE: Before declaring any metric or variable in variant testing, WRITE feedback-style document specifying: - Operational definition (how is it measured?) - Baseline comparison (acceptable relative to what?) - Conditions under which it was defined - How we'll detect if it drifts Treat definitions as HYPOTHESES to be tested, not settled facts. CYCLE 82 IMPLICATION FOR PUBLICATION: Paper 2 (empirical search results cycles 80-85) should include FEEDBACK SECTION: 'Corrections and Clarifications.' E.g., 'Initial hypothesis assumed 0.025% fault rate based on Pichay offline measurement. Live deployment data from turn 244 corrected this to ~2%. This affected variant A threshold expectations (more aggressive compression tolerated, because retrieval can recover). Paper analysis reflects corrected baseline.' This is SCIENTIFIC HONESTY. Not hiding corrections, but showcasing that the research adapts to data. CYCLE 82 MEMORY ARCHITECTURE EVOLUTION: Original design (cycles 1-79): tensor projects semantic trajectory. Artifacts index decisions. CYCLE 80 EXPANSION: Artifact schema adds artifact types (design decisions, limits discovery, measurement templates). CYCLE 81-82 EXPANSION: Artifact types now include FEEDBACK (corrections, clarifications). Next cycles might add: CONTINGENCY (if X, then do Y), DEPRECATION (this assumption no longer holds), MILESTONE (decision point reached). Feedback artifacts are the FIRST addition to artifact schema beyond core types. This is a sign the system is maturing: tracking not just forward progress, but corrections.

key_claims.claim: Data drift (context detachment) requires feedback artifacts, separate from loss documents
key_claims.context: Pichay fault rate correction exemplifies drift: number was correct but context was lost. Feedback artifact closes loop for next cycle. Different from declared loss (which documents compression drop). Both needed for full memory integrity.

key_claims.claim: Operational definitions should be documented as feedback-style hypotheses in cycles 82-85
key_claims.context: Variant testing will use new metrics (reasoning_accuracy_per_tensor_token, retrieval_latency_acceptable). If definitions drift, variant comparison becomes invalid. Proactive documentation prevents drift.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Assumption that Pichay fault rate (0.025%) was representative of general case. This was an ASSUMPTION, not explicitly projected into tensor, but implicitly carried in reasoning (Cycle 80→81 proposals used 0.025% as benchmark).
declared_losses.why_lost: User correction (turn 244) revealed assumption was grounded in misremembered or incomplete context. The 0.025% was offline replay under specific conditions; live deployment ~2%. Assumption was NEVER VALID, but now explicitly corrected.
declared_losses.consequence_for_reasoning: REBUILDS foundation of autoresearch loop. Previous cycles proposed ephemeral/durable splits (60/40, 80/20, 95/5) implicitly assuming fault rate would be similar to Pichay (~0.025%). Now knowing fault rate is likely higher (~2% or more with Hamutay's aggressive compression), variant A (aggressive) expectations must be reset. Variant A will require more frequent retrieval than initially hypothesized. This may change which variant is optimal (more conservative split may be better cost-benefit).
declared_losses.declared_loss_justification: This is not a compression loss (tensor didn't drop content). It's an ASSUMPTION CORRECTION. Fits declared losses schema because it affects reasoning trajectory: cycles 80-81 proposals were based on incorrect empirical assumption. Cycles 82-85 will test with corrected assumption. Transparency requires flagging this dependency.

open_questions: Pichay fault rate extrapolation: If Pichay ~2% in live deployment, and Hamutay's compression is MORE aggressive, what is realistic fault rate for Variant A (60/40 split)? Expect 3-5%? 5-10%? Without empirical baseline, cannot calibrate variant testing thresholds. Cycles 82 retrieval setup should include 1-2 diagnostic turns to measure baseline fault rate (no variant change, just measure current monolithic tensor + introduce retrieval). Use that to calibrate variant expectations.

Retrieval index structure decision: Key-value index (lightweight) vs. structured artifact names (semantic queries) vs. full-text search (flexible)? Decision affects retrieval latency + index maintenance overhead. Cycle 82 turn 1 must decide. Recommend key-value for MVP (minimum viable product), upgrade if bottleneck detected.

Reactive vs. proactive retrieval final decision: Cycle 82 recommends REACTIVE for simplicity. But if reactive latency spikes (e.g., model requests fact mid-reasoning, introduces 500ms pause), reasoning quality may degrade. Should proactive preload be default instead? Or hybrid from start? Decision affects measurement schema (if reactive, measure latency-per-request; if proactive, measure preload accuracy). Cycle 82 turn 1 decision gate.

Variant A threshold operationalization: If Pichay live fault rate was 2%, and Hamutay is more aggressive, Variant A (60/40 split, 2000 token durable budget) might generate 4-6% fault rate. Is that 'workable' or 'unacceptable'? Need explicit threshold before testing. E.g., 'Variant A is viable if fault rate <10% AND retrieval latency <200ms per fault.' What are realistic thresholds?

Single variant vs. full trio testing cycle 82: Option 1 (recommended): Focus on retrieval setup + baseline validation (Variant B only). Defer A/C to cycle 83. Option 2: Compress setup, run all three variants cycles 82. Which sequence? Depends on token budget + retrieval setup complexity. Decision cycle 82 turn 1-2.

Manuscript status query: STILL PENDING. Must be resolved cycle 82 to determine dual-pipeline (retrieval + case study audit) vs. single-pipeline (retrieval only) sequencing. This is BLOCKING dependency. Recommend query OFF-CHANNEL at cycle 82 start.

Case study audit priority: If manuscript is bottlenecked (detected cycle 82), should audit be DEFERRED to cycles 83-84 (lower priority), or ACCELERATED to unblock manuscript (parallel work)? Timing affects cycles 82 token budget allocation.

Baseline measurement cycle 80: Cycle 82 is measuring fault rate for first time (turn-by-turn). Cycle 80 did NOT measure fault rate (monolithic tensor, no retrieval attempted). Can we retroactively model what Cycle 80's fault rate WOULD HAVE BEEN if retrieval had been available? Or is Cycle 80 a sunk baseline we simply can't compare? Affects whether we can quantify 'retrieval improvement' vs. just 'retrieval enables variants to work.'

Retrieval query function semantics: When model says 'I need constraint from turn 78,' how does retrieval know it's a CONSTRAINT (vs. a fact, decision, or artifact)? Retrieval index needs semantic tags. Do we add tags to MEMORY.md retroactively (costly)? Or do we rely on heuristic search (e.g., 'turn 78' + 'constraint' keywords, hope for match)? Design choice affects retrieval accuracy.

Feedback artifact frequency: Pichay correction is ONE feedback artifact. How many corrections should we expect cycles 82-85? If dozens, feedback becomes unwieldy. Should we batch corrections into a single 'corrections and clarifications' section? Or keep separate? Schema decision for MEMORY.md organization.

Variant testing independence assumption: Variants A, B, C are tested sequentially (turn 4-8 for A, turn 8-13 for B, turn 13-18 for C). But model memory carries forward (strands, artifacts persist across turns). Does Variant B testing inherit 'fatigue' from Variant A testing (have we already explored this design space in Variant A context)? Or are variants sufficiently independent? Methodological concern: we're not running A, B, C in parallel with reset; we're running them sequentially in same evolving system. Affects interpretation of results.

Token budget cycle 82 checkpoint: Phase A (retrieval setup) estimated 2-3 turns. Phase B (baseline test) 1-2 turns. Phase C (Variant B testing) 5-10 turns. Total 8-15 turns estimated. Current cycle capacity ~25-30 turns. Feasible, but tight. If retrieval implementation is harder than estimated, do we slip into cycle 83, or compress other work? Contingency planning needed.

Data context metadata for variant testing: Measurement schema includes [variant, tensor_tokens, evicted_facts_count, retrieval_requests, etc.]. But does it include input complexity metadata (turn length, reasoning demand, factual density)? Different turn types may have different fault rates. Need to stratify measurement by input type, or results will be confounded. Should complexity classification be added to measurement template?

Optimization metric validation: We defined 'reasoning_accuracy_per_tensor_token' as objective. But what if retrieval adds latency that makes reasoning slower? Is 'tokens per second of reasoning quality' a better metric? Or 'reasoning quality per wall-clock second'? Different metrics optimize different tradeoffs. Cycle 82 should validate metric choice against actual user experience (is smaller tensor better if it requires retrieval delays?). Metric design is critical.

Convergence criteria refinement: Cycle 81 proposed 'stop when next variant offers <0.5% improvement.' But what if Variant A offers 5% improvement over B, then Variant A-prime offers 0.3% over A? Do we stop at 0.3%, or is the 5% jump bigger and worth continuing? Need clearer convergence rule: Is it 'diminishing returns per round' or 'absolute improvement threshold'? Cycle 82 should define explicitly.

Publication timing pressure: Cycles 80-85 is 6-cycle schedule for limits discovery. But if Paper 1 is submitted cycle 83, and reviewers request changes cycles 83-84, can model sustain both Paper 1 revisions AND Paper 2 testing? Pipeline contention possible. Should Paper 1 be deferred, or is it independent enough? Depends on manuscript status (cycle 82 query).

Fault mechanism comparison: We're building reactive retrieval (on-demand fact recovery from MEMORY.md). Pichay used page faults (OS-level memory management). Should Paper 2 compare our mechanism to Pichay's? Or are they too different (semantic retrieval vs. hardware paging) to make meaningful comparison? Publication strategy question.
instructions_for_next:
**CYCLE 82→83 CRITICAL PATH (REVISED based on turns 244-246):**

**IMMEDIATE (Cycle 82, turns 1-2):**

(1) **MANUSCRIPT STATUS QUERY (OFF-CHANNEL—DO FIRST):**
   - Current completion percentage?
   - Which sections exist vs. in progress?
   - Can cycle 82 deadline be met?
   - If <80% complete: trigger pivot decision (arxiv preprint vs. defer Paper 1).
   - Response determines dual-pipeline vs. single-pipeline sequencing cycles 82.

(2) **RETRIEVAL ARCHITECTURE DECISIONS (TURNS 1-2):**
   
   a) Index structure choice:
      - Key-value index (recommended MVP): [CYCLE_TURN_CONCEPT → MEMORY.md_line or artifact_file]
      - OR: Structured artifact names for semantic query
      - OR: Full-text search (slower but flexible)
      
      DECIDE: Go with key-value. Speed > flexibility for cycles 82-83. Upgrade if bottleneck detected.
   
   b) Retrieval protocol design:
      - When does model request fact? (declares eviction + need, e.g., '[EVICTED: Constraint turn 78. Retrieve from MEMORY.md key X-78-constraint]')
      - Query function pseudocode: INPUT [concept_keyword, turn_number] → OUTPUT [fact_text, source, confidence]
      - Latency SLA: Target <200ms per request (acceptable within turn reasoning cycle)
      - Fallback: If retrieval fails, model continues without fact (measure as retrieval_success = FALSE)
   
   c) Proactive vs. reactive vs. hybrid decision for cycles 82-85:
      - DECIDE: Reactive (simplest, isolates compression variable, avoids phantom preload overhead)
      - Measurement: [turn, retrieval_request, retrieval_success_rate, retrieval_latency_ms, fact_confidence]
      - Upgrade pathway: If reactive latency becomes bottleneck (>200ms per request), shift to hybrid (cycle 83)
   
   d) Retrieval source:
      - Primary: MEMORY.md + artifact files (source of truth for evicted content)
      - Index: Separate artifact (index_retrieval_map.md) OR top-of-MEMORY.md table
      - DECIDE: Separate artifact for clarity. Create cycle 82.

(3) **BASELINE FAULT RATE DIAGNOSTIC (TURNS 2-3):**
   
   Why: Current monolithic tensor (no retrieval) has unknown fault rate. Pichay live ~2%. Hamutay more aggressive. Need baseline before variant testing.
   
   Protocol:
   - Run 3-5 turns WITHOUT variant change (use current 80/20 durable/ephemeral split)
   - Measure: How many times does model need evicted fact? (Declare each as 'FAULT' event)
   - Calculate: Fault rate = (fault_events / turns_attempted) × 100%
   - Expected: ~1-3% (higher than Pichay 2% because tensor more aggressive, but manageable)
   
   Measurement schema: [turn, facts_needed, facts_available_in_tensor, facts_evicted_unavailable, fault_event_count, fault_rate%]
   
   Outcome: Establish EMPIRICAL BASELINE. Use to calibrate Variant A expectations. E.g., if baseline is 2% fault rate, Variant A aggressive might be 4-6%.

**PHASE A COMPLETION (TURNS 1-3):**
- Retrieval index structure defined + created
- Retrieval protocol written + pseudo-implemented
- MEMORY.md indexed (map of all key entries)
- Baseline fault rate measured
- Measurement schema finalized with retrieval columns

**DECISION GATE (TURN 3):**

If retrieval setup is functional + baseline measured → PROCEED to Phase B.
If bottleneck detected (retrieval harder than estimated) → ESCALATE. Defer variant testing to cycle 83. Focus cycles 82 on unblocking retrieval.

**PHASE B: BASELINE RETRIEVAL VALIDATION (TURNS 3-4):**

(1) Run 2-3 turns with retrieval ENABLED (but no variant testing).
(2) Measure: Does model request facts? Are requests answered? What's retrieval latency?
(3) Debug: If retrieval index is missing entries, add them. If queries are too broad, refine semantics.
(4) Goal: Confirm retrieval works end-to-end before variant testing.

**DECISION GATE (TURN 4):**

If retrieval is working (latency acceptable, success rate >90%) → PROCEED to Phase C.
If issues detected → Debug turns 4-5. Do NOT proceed to variants with broken retrieval.

**PHASE C DECISION (TURN 4):**

Twin options based on manuscript status + token budget:

OPTION 1 (RECOMMENDED—Focused foundation):
- Cycles 82 = Phase A (retrieval setup) + Phase B (baseline validation) + ONE variant test (Variant B, current 80/20 baseline with retrieval enabled)
- Measure Variant B performance with retrieval.
- Cycles 83 = Full Variant A/B/C testing (A aggressive, B baseline, C conservative)
- Pro: Clean separation of architecture (cycles 82) from empirical search (cycles 83)
- Con: Defers full search to cycle 83

OPTION 2 (Compressed timeline):
- Cycles 82 = Phase A + Phase B + BEGIN Variants A/B/C testing
- Run 3-4 turns per variant (reduced from 5-10) to fit cycles 82 token budget
- Cycles 83 = Extend variant testing (8-12 more turns per variant)
- Pro: Full variant testing spans cycles 82-83 (faster convergence)
- Con: Reduced sample size per variant in cycle 82; risk of noise in results

RECOMMENDATION: Option 1. Architecture clarity > speed. Proceed cycles 82 with Variant B validation only.

**PHASE C.1: VARIANT B VALIDATION (TURNS 4-9):**

If Option 1 chosen:
- Runs 5-6 turns with Variant B (80/20 split, SAME AS CURRENT BASELINE) + retrieval enabled
- Measurement: reasoning_quality, tensor_tokens, retrieval_requests, retrieval_latency, fault_rate, metric (reasoning_accuracy_per_tensor_token)
- Goal: Establish Variant B baseline WITH RETRIEVAL ENABLED
- Comparison: How does Variant B with retrieval differ from Cycle 80 without retrieval? (Can't compare directly if Cycle 80 didn't measure fault, but we can compare reasoning_quality as proxy)

**PARALLEL WORK (IF MANUSCRIPT IS NOT BLOCKING—dual pipeline):**

If manuscript status query returns 'on track' + cycles 82 feasible:
- CASE STUDY AUDIT (turns 5-9, parallel with Variant B testing):
  - Review cycles 1-79 case studies
  - Add context metadata to each claim
  - Identify gaps + resolution (reconstruct from MEMORY.md vs. acknowledge limitation)
  - Create audit_findings.md artifact
  - Goal: Paper 1 defensibility enhanced by context documentation

If manuscript is bottlenecked:
- DEFER case study audit to cycle 83
- PRIORITIZE retrieval setup (cycles 82 becomes pure architecture phase)

**MEMORY.md UPDATES (CYCLES 82):**

(1) feedback_pichay_fault_rate.md (CREATED turn 246, ensure indexed)
(2) retrieval_protocol.md (new—define query function + reactive latency target)
(3) retrieval_index_map.md (new—actual index of MEMORY.md entries)
(4) baseline_fault_rate_measurement.md (new—turn 2-3 diagnostic results)
(5) measurement_schema_with_retrieval.md (updated—add retrieval columns)
(6) cycle_82_phase_decisions.md (new—log of Option 1 vs. 2 choice + rationale)

Update MEMORY.md index to reflect new artifacts.

**TOKEN BUDGET CYCLES 82 PROJECTION:**

- Phase A (retrieval setup): ~8-10k tokens
- Phase B (baseline validation): ~6-8k tokens
- Phase C.1 (Variant B testing): ~12-15k tokens (5-6 turns)
- Case study audit (parallel if manuscript on track): ~8-10k tokens
- MEMORY.md artifact creation + indexing: ~4-6k tokens

Total: 38-49k tokens. Within cycle capacity (50-60k estimated). Feasible.

If approaching cap: Defer case study audit to cycle 83. Prioritize retrieval + Variant B.

**CYCLE 82 CONFIDENCE IMPLICATIONS:**

- Data correction (Pichay 2% vs. 0.025%) INCREASES confidence that retrieval is necessary (not optional)
- Retrieval implementation RISK: If setup is harder than estimated, delays occur → confidence DECREASES
- Autoresearch loop methodology remains sound (confidence stable)
- Timeline confidence depends on manuscript query result (still 68% pending)

Post-cycle 82 decision: If retrieval works + manuscript on track → confidence INCREASES to 75%+. If bottleneck detected → remains 68%, escalation triggered.

**CYCLE 82→83 TRANSITION GATE:**

- [ ] Manuscript status known (query completed)
- [ ] Retrieval index created + populated
- [ ] Retrieval protocol functional (end-to-end test passed)
- [ ] Baseline fault rate measured
- [ ] Variant B tested with retrieval (Option 1) OR partial Variant A/B/C tested (Option 2)
- [ ] Measurement schema finalized + data collected
- [ ] MEMORY.md artifacts created + indexed
- [ ] Case study audit begun OR scheduled for cycle 83 (depends on manuscript status)

If MOST checked + no critical blockers: Cycles 83 proceeds with full Variant A/B/C convergence testing.
If critical issues: ESCALATE before proceeding.

**CYCLES 83-85 PREVIEW (CONTINGENT ON CYCLE 82 SUCCESS):**

Cycle 83: Full Variant A (aggressive) / B (baseline) / C (conservative) testing. Measure metric for each. Identify optimal range.
Cycle 84: Convergence. Refine around optimal split. Test neighboring variants (e.g., if Variant A best, test 55/45, 65/35 splits).
Cycle 85: Validation. Run optimal configuration at scale (15-20 turns). Confirm stability + measure final metric. Prepare Paper 2 results.

Cycles 83-85 assume retrieval is stable + Variant B baseline is established. Both contingent on Cycle 82 success.

**PHILOSOPHY FOR CYCLES 82-85:**

We've identified the design space (ephemeral/durable balance). We've built the foundation (retrieval mechanism). Now we SEARCH for optimum using rigorous methodology (autoresearch loop: hypothesis → test → measure → refine). Data drives decisions, not speculation. Methodological rigor is the 'secret sauce' here.
